beats.prompt: Quick warm-up from L2 — match each conditional to which direction it points.

feedback.correct: P(positive | sick) is how good the test is; P(sick | positive) is what you actually want — and they're different. Same swap is the whole of today's lesson.
hints: From L2: a 99% test on a 1% disease still left you at 50%.
One side describes the evidence (forward); one is the belief you want (backward).
Accuracy of the evidence ≠ probability of the hypothesis.

beats.prompt: A DNA profile matches a suspect. The lab says the profile occurs in 1 in a million people. The suspect was found by searching a database of 10,000 others. What is the chance he's innocent?

options: About 1 in a million — the match is that rare
You can't tell without the size of the suspect pool

About 1 in a million — the match is that.note: That's P(match | innocent) read backward. With 10,000 other innocents who could match by chance, the odds are 100:1 → he's the source 100/101, innocent only 1/101.

You can't tell without the size of the s.note: Exactly — the pool is the hidden prior. A pool of 10,001 possible sources puts him at 100/101, not 999,999/1,000,000.

Basically certain he's guilty.note: Strong, but not one-in-a-million-certain. The cold-hit pool leaves a real 1/101 chance he's innocent.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll flip the conditional.
1-in-a-million is P(match | innocent), not P(innocent | match).

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on forward vs backward conditionals — then continue.

interaction.title: Forward vs backward
interaction.body: P(evidence | hypothesis) is not P(hypothesis | evidence). 'One in a million innocents match' (forward) is not 'one in a million chance he's innocent' (backward). To flip the arrow you need Bayes — and the prior (here, the size of the suspect pool you searched).

feedback.correct: P(E|H) reads forward (evidence given a hypothesis); P(H|E) reads backward (hypothesis given the evidence). They are different numbers.
hints: The conditional bar | has a direction.
'1 in a million innocents match' is forward; 'he's innocent with prob 1 in a million' is backward.
Bayes is the machine that flips forward into backward — using the prior.

beats.prompt: Watch P(source | match) settle at 100/101 — strong, but a thousandfold weaker than 'one in a million.'

hero.structuralReadout: 1 true source vs ~1/100 expected false matches among 10,000 innocents → odds 100:1 → 100/101.

feedback.correct: One true source plus about one coincidental match among 10,000 innocents → odds 100:1 → P(source) = 100/101 ≈ 99%. Strong evidence — but a thousandfold weaker than 'one in a million.'
hints: The match is near-certain for the true source and one-in-a-million for each innocent.
But there are ~10,000 innocents, so ~1/100 of a coincidental match is expected.

beats.prompt: From the pool of 10,001 possible sources: compute P(innocent | match) and then P(he is the source | match).

feedback.correct: From the pool: P(innocent | match) = 1/101, so P(source | match) = 100/101. The 1-in-a-million was P(match | innocent) — the forward number; flipping it through the pool lands at 1/101 backward.
hints: Among ~101 expected matches (1 true + ~100 coincidental over the whole pool), how many are innocent?
About 1 of every 101 matches is innocent → P(innocent | match) = 1/101.

beats.prompt: Same 1-in-a-million match, two different priors. Compute P(source | match) in each case.
beats.interviewNote: The prosecutor's fallacy / cold-hit DNA is a canonical quant/stats interview question (interview pack `showcase-prosecutors-fallacy`). The clean frame is odds: posterior odds = prior odds × likelihood ratio. The match's LR (1,000,000) never changes; the searched pool is the prior, and it alone decides whether the hit means 100/101 or 1/2.

feedback.correct: Same 1-in-a-million match, two priors: a cold hit (1 of 10,001) → 100/101; probable cause first (prior 1/2) → 1,000,000/1,000,001 ≈ 1. The match's probative value is set by the prior, not the match alone.
hints: The likelihood ratio (1,000,000) is identical in both — only the prior odds differ.

beats.prompt: Match each scenario to the backward read it hides.

pairs.right: not the same as P(clouds | rain)

feedback.correct: Same backward read in three costumes — DNA, medicine, weather. Each confuses P(E|H) with P(H|E).
hints: Each statement quotes a forward conditional as if it were the backward one.
Match by which arrow is given vs which is claimed.
DNA: P(match|innocent); medical: P(+|sick); weather: P(rain|clouds) — none is its flip.

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on 100/101 — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Pool ×100
lenses.body: Among ~101 expected matches in the pool, 100 are the (one) true source's vs ~1 innocent → 100/101

feedback.correct: The Bayes formula, the odds ratio, and the 'blow the pool up ×100' picture all give 100/101 — not a rounding of one-in-a-million.
hints: Reveal each lens.
They are three roads to the same number.
All three say 100/101.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: a cold hit found by trawling a database of 1,000,001 people; the same 1-in-1,000,000 random-match profile. What is P(he is the source | match)?

interaction.scenario: A cold hit found by trawling a database of 1,000,001 people; the same 1-in-1,000,000 random-match profile.

feedback.correct: Use the odds form of Bayes: \[ \text{posterior odds} = \text{prior odds}\times\text{likelihood ratio} \] Database: 1,000,001 people (1 true source \(+\) 1,000,000 innocents). Prior odds \(= 1:1{,}000{,}000\). Likelihood ratio \(= \frac{P(\text{match}\mid\text{source})}{P(\text{match}\mid\text{innocent})} = \frac{1}{1/1{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}000{,}000\). Substituting: \[ \text{posterior odds} = \frac{1}{1{,}000{,}000}\times 1{,}000{,}000 = 1{:}1 \] Odds \(1:1\) gives \(P(\text{source}\mid\text{match}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\). A larger database means more expected coincidental matches — a cold hit grows weaker, not stronger, as the search pool expands.
hints: Prior odds \(= 1:1{,}000{,}000\) (1 true source vs 1,000,000 innocents in the database).
Likelihood ratio \(= \frac{P(\text{match}\mid\text{source})}{P(\text{match}\mid\text{innocent})} = \frac{1}{1/1{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}000{,}000\).
Posterior odds \(= \frac{1}{1{,}000{,}000}\times 1{,}000{,}000 = 1{:}1\), giving \(P(\text{source}\mid\text{match}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to consolidate the directional lesson.

feedback.correct: P(E|H) ≠ P(H|E): the conditional points one way. Flip it with Bayes — and restore the prior (the pool) you forgot.
hints: '1 in a million innocents match' is not '1 in a million chance he's innocent' → 100/101.
The match's LR is fixed; the searched pool is the prior that sets its meaning.
Next up: spotting base-rate neglect in the wild — name the base rate, then update.
